topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the blunt finality of a closing door. "Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise party for Sarah is cancelled." I reread the message three times, my thumb hovering over the 'reply' button before finally clicking 'delete.' My shoulders slumped, a heavy, dead weight settling across my back. I stared at the screen, the cheerful invitations and planned decorations from weeks ago mocking me. *Unforeseen circumstances.* That was the kind of vague, corporate language they used when things went sideways. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I didn't bother looking at my phone; I just needed a moment to process the sheer absurdity of it all. Maybe they just changed their minds. Maybe it was too much trouble. I stood up, walked to the window, and watched the unremarkable afternoon traffic flow by, feeling a strange, cool detachment settle over me.

[story 2]

"Cancelled." The single word echoed in the silence of my apartment. I picked up my lukewarm coffee mug, the ceramic feeling suddenly slick in my hand. I tapped the screen of my laptop repeatedly, half-expecting the notification to magically change, to reveal some hidden clause or a typo. My jaw tightened. I paced the small living room, the sound of my worn slippers scuffing the hardwood floor amplifying the hollow thudding in my chest. Who would cancel a party? It felt like a deliberate maneuver, a calculated move designed to cause maximum disruption. I stopped by the bookshelf, running a finger over the spines of old novels, searching for some hidden clue, some explanation buried in the narrative. Nothing. Just the undeniable fact sitting there, cold and hard. I sighed, a long, slow expulsion of air, and turned back to the blank screen, unconvinced by the neat little explanation offered.

[story 3]

I stared at the confirmation text, my eyes narrowing until they felt like slits. The details—the venue booked, the catering confirmed, the coordinated efforts of half the office—all evaporated into meaningless noise. A slow, tight knot formed in my stomach, a physical reaction to the sudden deflation. I picked up my phone again, scrolling back through the thread of messages, searching for any hint of a retraction, any coded message suggesting this was a bluff. My breathing became shallow, quick catches of air. I didn't respond immediately. Instead, I walked into the kitchen, deliberately pouring myself another glass of water, the ice clinking loudly in the quiet room. It was either a massive oversight, or something much more deliberate. I took a long sip, my gaze fixed on the condensation tracing paths down the glass, refusing to accept the first, flimsy explanation offered.
